topic: A close reading of long takes and landscapes in Terrence Malick's 'The Tree of Life' and 'Days of Heaven,' exploring how visual patience became a moral stance in American cinema.
outline_hint: Title slide — 'The Patience of Light: Long Takes in Malick'
Why patience matters — the moral weight of looking longer
Days of Heaven — the golden hour as cinematic ethics
The harvest sequence — a close reading of one 4-minute take
The Tree of Life — cosmic scope and domestic intimacy
The creation sequence — beauty as theology
Stillness vs. silence — what the camera does when nothing happens
Influences: Ozu, Bresson, Tarkovsky — a brief comparative lens
The legacy — how Malick reshaped independent American cinema
Discussion prompt: Is slowness a privilege? Who gets to be patient?
Closing still + recommended reading list

topic: A thematic dissection of 'The Remains of the Day' by Kazuo Ishiguro — the architecture of emotional repression, the unreliability of memory, and what Stevens the butler cannot say.

The wartime correspondence between Simone Weil and Albert Camus (1942–1947) — two moral philosophers who never met in person but shaped each other's final thinking on suffering, solidarity, and the duty to act.
outline_hint: Title slide — 'Letters Across the Abyss'
Who was Simone Weil? — a brief biographical sketch
Who was Albert Camus? — a brief biographical sketch
How the correspondence began — Camus publishes Weil posthumously
On suffering — where they agreed
On the duty to act — where they diverged
Weil's concept of 'attention' vs. Camus's concept of 'revolt'
Timeline of their parallel lives — visual chronology
What each philosopher took from the other
Why their exchange still matters — moral philosophy in a fractured age

topic: 'On the Loom' — a curatorial walk-through of Anni Albers's textile experiments at Black Mountain College, tracing how one weaver's refusal to separate craft from art changed American modernism.
